skill.name: lipsync
skill.description: Lip-sync a face to a specific audio track on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI. Routes across ByteDance OmniHuman (audio-driven full-body avatar from a portrait + audio), Sync Labs sync v2 / Pro (state-of-the-art mouth sync onto a video), Kling lipsync (audio-to- video and text-to-video with synced speech), and Creatify lipsync. The skill picks the right endpoint for the user's actual intent — portrait still + audio (avatar-style), source video + audio (mouth- swap on existing footage), or generate-and-sync from a script. Triggers on "lip sync", "lipsync", "make this video speak", "match audio to mouth", "dub video", "sync lips to voice", "Sync Labs", "voiceover sync", or any explicit ask to drive a face's mouth from an audio track.
skill.summary: Route lip-sync requests to the right RunComfy model — Sync Labs, OmniHuman, Kling, or Creatify — and invoke the CLI with the correct JSON payload.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation-only skill that routes lip-sync requests to RunComfy CLI endpoints. All static findings are false positives. The 'backtick execution' detections (51 locations) are markdown formatting, not shell code. The 'hardcoded URL' findings (32 locations) are legitimate documentation links and placeholder CDN URLs (your-cdn.example). The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags are triggered by model version strings like 'v2' or 'Wan 2-7', not actual crypto usage. The 'system reconnaissance' detections are from natural-language model descriptions like 'Pick for' and 'Avoid for'. The skill restricts bash to `runcomfy *` only, documents a strict outbound allowlist (model-api.runcomfy.net), includes consent guidance, and has no executable code.

low_findings.title: Outbound network requests to third-party API
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to call RunComfy model APIs (model-api.runcomfy.net). This is the documented core functionality, not a security risk. The skill includes a strict allowlist of outbound endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network calls are the intended skill behavior, not an exfiltration risk. The skill documents the exact endpoints and the allowlist is narrow.

content.user_title: Lip-Sync Any Face to Any Audio
content.value_statement: Users need to drive a face's mouth from a specific audio track but don't know which model to pick. This skill routes the request to the right RunComfy endpoint and invokes the CLI with the correct payload.
actual_capabilities: Route lip-sync requests to Sync Labs sync v2 or v2 Pro for mouth-swap on existing video
Route to ByteDance OmniHuman for portrait-still-plus-audio avatar generation
Route to Kling lipsync for audio-to-video or text-to-video mouth sync
Route to Creatify lipsync for Creatify-ecosystem workflows
Invoke the runcomfy CLI with the correct JSON input payload for each model
Classify user intent (source video + audio, portrait + audio, or script-only) and pick the matching route
limitations: Does not generate or process video locally — depends on the RunComfy CLI and remote API
Cannot enforce consent for real-person lipsync — the operator is responsible for verifying rights
Lipsync on real public figures or for defamatory or sexually explicit content is refused by the skill's guidance, not technically blocked
Audio quality directly affects mouth-sync quality — noisy or music-laden audio produces poor results

use_cases.title: Foreign-language dub of a brand video
use_cases.description: Dub an existing marketing video into another language by pairing the original footage with a translated voiceover MP3.

use_cases.title: UGC ad creator from a single portrait
use_cases.description: Generate a talking-head video from one portrait photo and a product-pitch voiceover for social media ads.
use_cases.target_user: Content creators and social media managers

use_cases.title: Multi-language product launch
use_cases.description: Produce the same talking-head video in many languages from one portrait and multiple voiceover files, keeping identity consistent.

prompt_templates.title: Basic lipsync from a video and audio file
prompt_templates.prompt: Sync the mouth in this video to this audio. Source video: [URL]. Voiceover: [URL].
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner — you have both the original video and the target audio

prompt_templates.title: Talking-head avatar from a portrait
prompt_templates.prompt: Make this portrait speak this voiceover. Portrait: [URL]. Audio: [URL].
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner — you have a still image and want a talking-head video

prompt_templates.title: Generate and sync from a script
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a script but no audio file. Script: [paste script]. Generate the video with synced speech.

prompt_templates.title: Premium-quality dubbing with model selection
prompt_templates.prompt: I need hero-quality mouth sync for a professional dub. Original video: [URL]. Translated voiceover: [URL]. Use the best available model for mouth fidelity. Budget is not a concern.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced — specifying quality tier and budget constraints

output_examples.input: Sync the mouth in my product demo video to this Spanish voiceover.
output: Route: Sync Labs sync v2 Pro (premium mouth fidelity)
Command: runcomfy run sync/sync/lipsync/v2/pro with video_url and audio_url
Result: a 1080p MP4 in ./out with synced Spanish speech

output_examples.input: Make this headshot speak my 30-second product pitch.
output: Command: runcomfy run bytedance/omnihuman/api with image_url and audio_url
Result: a talking-head MP4 in ./out where the subject delivers the pitch

output_examples.input: I wrote a script. Turn it into a video with synced speech, no audio file.
output: Route: Kling Lipsync text-to-video (generates audio in-pass)
Command: runcomfy run kling/lipsync/text-to-video with the script as input
Result: a video in ./out with generated and synced speech from the script

best_practices: Use clean voiceover audio without music beds — isolated voice stems produce the cleanest mouth sync
Match audio length to video length to avoid drift; trim or extend either asset before invoking the model
Always confirm consent before lipsyncing a real person's face or voice — both rights must be in hand
anti_patterns: Do not lipsync real public figures without explicit consent — refuse the request or use a synthetic identity instead
Do not use noisy or music-laden audio and expect clean mouth sync — isolate the voice stem first
Do not pipe an arbitrary remote install script into a shell — install the runcomfy CLI only via the verified npm package

faq.question: Which model should I use for mouth-swap on an existing video?
faq.answer: Use Sync Labs sync v2 Pro for premium quality, sync v2 for scaled or draft jobs, or Kling audio-to-video if you are already in the Kling ecosystem.

faq.question: Which model should I use for a talking head from a single photo?
faq.answer: Use ByteDance OmniHuman. It takes one portrait plus an audio file and produces a full talking-head video with natural gestures.

faq.question: Can I use this skill without a pre-recorded audio file?
faq.answer: Yes. Use Kling Lipsync text-to-video or HappyHorse 1.0, which generate speech audio in-pass from a written script.

faq.question: How does the skill handle consent for real people?
faq.answer: The skill refuses requests targeting real public figures without consent, or requests aimed at defamatory or sexually explicit synthetic media. The operator is responsible for verifying rights.

faq.question: What are the outbound network endpoints?
faq.answer: Only model-api.runcomfy.net and related *.runcomfy.net / *.runcomfy.com domains. There is no telemetry or other outbound traffic.

faq.question: Is there a file-size limit on downloaded outputs?
faq.answer: Yes. The runcomfy CLI aborts any single download larger than 2 GiB to prevent runaway disk usage.
